serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Prawn Jhalfrezi is approximately 300 g, comprising shell-off prawns in a tomato-based gravy with vegetables, served as a main course alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn / Shrimp curry composite; ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Prawn Jhalfrezi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN 2017. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN entries for 'Prawn curry' (cooked prawns in spiced tomato-onion gravy) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Shrimp, cooked' (FDC ID 175180) and 'Tomato-based curry sauce' components. Macros reflect a cooked dish with approximately 40% prawn by weight, 30% tomato-onion gravy, and 30% mixed vegetables (bell pepper, onion). Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood curry with tomato-vegetable base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Jhalfrezi. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed seafood and vegetable curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 30–45 for protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate curry dishes with tomato-vegetable base). The dish is high in protein and low in carbohydrate (4.8 g/100g), which suppresses glycemic response. GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 14.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 5.0. Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 300 g restaurant serving of Prawn Jhalfrezi may contribute approximately 1260 mg sodium (over 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~4.8 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Jhalfrezi has a low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a low glycemic load per serving (~5), making it a suitable protein-rich main course for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is paired with a low-GI staple and sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~115 mg/100g) from prawns, partially offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol (~115 mg/100g) but low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Prawn Jhalfrezi in moderate portions (150–200 g) while monitoring overall dietary cholesterol and sodium intake, and should prefer preparations using minimal oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Jhalfrezi is a popular Indian seafood dish consisting of shell-off prawns stir-fried and simmered in a semi-dry, spiced tomato-based gravy with onions, bell peppers, green chillies, ginger, and garlic. The dish originates from the Jhalfrezi cooking tradition of Bengal and North India, where 'jhal' means spicy and 'frezi' (from the Persian 'farz') refers to stir-frying. The cooking method involves sautéing aromatics in oil, adding tomatoes and spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder), then stir-frying the prawns until just cooked to preserve their tender, slightly firm texture. The result is a vibrant, moderately spicy dish with a tangy tomato base, sweet bell pepper notes, and the natural sweetness of fresh prawns. Nutritionally, Prawn Jhalfrezi is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish (~11.5 g protein, ~4.8 g carbohydrate per 100g) with a low glycemic index (~35), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus from the prawns, and vitamin C and vitamin A from the tomato-vegetable base. The sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~420 mg/100g), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, Prawn Jhalfrezi is served at Indian restaurants as a main course, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets.

confidence_reason: Prawn Jhalfrezi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN 2017 or any other primary approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN prawn curry entries and USDA FoodData Central shrimp and curry component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate curry dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 'Prawn curry' and USDA FoodData Central shrimp/curry components; no exact match exists in any single approved source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source entries used for this dish. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entries for prawn curry. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as prawns and vegetable-based curry ingredients contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 5. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 based on comparable mixed seafood curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Prawn Jhalfrezi was found in the approved literature. 6. Sodium value (~420 mg/100g) reflects a typical restaurant preparation and may vary significantly depending on salt added during cooking. 7. Cholesterol value (~115 mg/100g) is consistent with the prawn content of the dish; prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol. 8. The dish is served at Mr Tandoor - Boat Quay (field survey data, SGD 23.90) as the source restaurant in the input record. 9. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this dish is served in Singapore's Indian restaurant context, following Singaporean multilingual naming rules.
